sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: While diffusion models generate high-fidelity video clips, transforming them into coherent storytelling engines remains challenging. Current agentic pipelines automate this via chained modules but suffer from semantic drift and cascading failures due to independent, handcrafted prompting. We present Co-Director, a hierarchical multi-agent framework formalizing video storytelling as a global optimization problem. To ensure semantic coherence, we introduce hierarchical parameterization: a multi-armed bandit globally identifies promising creative directions, while a local multimodal self-refinement loop mitigates identity drift and ensures sequence-level consistency. This balances the exploration of novel narrative strategies with the exploitation of effective creative configurations. For evaluation, we introduce GenAD-Bench, a 400-scenario dataset of fictional products for personalized advertising. Experiments demonstrate that Co-Director significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, offering a principled approach that seamlessly generalizes to broader cinematic narratives. Project Page: https://co-director-agent.github.io/

sections.title: Storytelling
sections.content: Yale Song1 *, Yiwen Song1, Nick Losier1, Nathan Hodson1, Ye Jin1, Rhyard Zhu1, Yan Xu1, Daniel Vlasic1, Carina Claassen1, Jasmine Leon1, Khanh G. LeViet1, Zack Chomyn1, Joe Timmons1, Brett Slatkin1, Scott Penberthy1 and Tomas Pfister1 1Google Inc. While diffusion models generate high-fidelity video clips, transforming them into coherent storytelling engines remains challenging. Current agentic pipelines automate this via chained modules but suffer from semantic drift and cascading failures due to independent, handcrafted prompting. We present Co-Director, hierarchical multi-agent framework formalizing video storytelling as global optimization problem. To ensure semantic coherence, we introduce hierarchical parameterization: multi-armed bandit globally identifies promising creative directions, while local multimodal self-refinement loop mitigates identity drift and ensures sequence-level consistency. This balances the exploration of novel narrative strategies with the exploitation of effective creative configurations. For evaluation, we introduce GenAd-Bench, 400-scenario dataset of fictional products for personalized advertising. Experiments demonstrate that Co-Director significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, offering principled approach that seamlessly generalizes to broader cinematic narratives. Project Page: https://co-director-agent.github.io/ 1. Introduction Generative video storytelling aims to transform abstract ideassuch as simple tagline and sparse reference visualsinto cohesive video narratives, like fully realized short film. It has diverse applications, ranging from democratizing high-end content creation (Divya and Mirza, 2024) to automating filmmaking (Zhang et al., 2025) and digital advertising (Baek, 2023). Realizing this potential requires jointly optimizing multiple objectives: narrative coherence, visual consistency, and mise-en-scènethe visual language dictating how camera angles, color palettes, and metaphors deliver the intended message (Zettl, 1973). Recent advancements in video diffusion demonstrate remarkable high-fidelity generation. Highprofile collaborations, such as Google DeepMinds work with Darren Aronofsky on Ancestra (Aronofsky and McNitt, 2025) and On This Day... 1776 (Aronofsky, 2026), showcase the artistic ceiling when backed by 200-person creative team. While representing the pinnacle of human-AI collaboration, such resources remain out of reach for independent creators. Without them, individuals must manually bridge the gap between LLM scripting and fragmented generative models, correcting for the consistency errors and identity drift inherent in current architectures (Elmoghany et al., 2025). To mitigate this, the community has pivoted toward agentic pipelines where LLMs orchestrate the multi-step generative process (Huang et al., 2025; Lin et al., 2023; Shi et al., 2025; Wei et al., 2025; Wu et al., 2025b). However, these systems remain susceptible to cascading failures, where early errors propagate and break long-horizon consistency (Zhou et al., 2024). This reflects the classical credit assignment problem (Minsky, 1961), as terminal failures are difficult to trace back to specific prompts. Furthermore, existing systems rely on static, handcrafted prompt templates lacking Corresponding author(s): yalesong@google.com Co-Director : Agentic Generative Video Storytelling unified creative vision. Without mechanism to map script-level changes to downstream generation, the resulting visual output often feels disjointed. Crucially, these approaches fail to capture the serendipity essential to creative production. Human directors often explore counter-intuitive ideas to find creative gold. Static, chained pipelines are fundamentally ill-equipped for such exploration, remaining anchored to fixed templates unable to adapt to the non-linear nature of creative success. In this work, we present Co-Director, multi-agent framework for automating video storytelling. To the best of our knowledge, Co-Director is the first system to formalize generative storytelling as global optimization problem. Unlike prior agentic pipelines that rely on linear, "waterfall" chains prone to semantic drift, we introduce hierarchical parameterization strategy steered by Multi-Armed Bandit (MAB) (Bubeck and Cesa-Bianchi, 2012). Rather than relying on fixed prompt templates, Co-Director utilizes MAB to sample abstract creative trajectories and dynamically injects these into the system prompts of sub-agents. This top-down steering ensures that all-agentsfrom script-writing to video generationare orthogonally aligned toward unified creative intent. This formulation formalizes the creative process as search for the optimal balance between explorationventuring into novel, unconventional narrative territoriesand exploitationrefining historically effective stylistic configurations. This allows Co-Director to move beyond simple self-correction toward principled exploration of the creative latent space, identifying the configuration that optimally balances visual fidelity with narrative effectiveness (Escalas, 2004). Furthermore, we argue that evaluating video storytelling requires shift from open-ended cinematic metrics towards rigorous, constraint-driven frameworks. We use digital advertising as our demonstration scenario because it demands both sophisticated storytelling (much like short cinematic film) and strict adherence to objective requirements: asset preservation, audience alignment, and value proposition communication (Teixeira et al., 2012). To this end, we introduce GenAd-Bench, dataset of 400 scenarios across 200 fictional products. By utilizing fictional entities, we respect copyright constraints while preventing models from defaulting to memorized training priors. We provide multimodal LLM (MLLM)-based evaluation suite covering Visual Asset Fidelity, Demographic Alignment, Visual Quality, and Marketing Appealanchored in the AIDA hierarchy (Barry and Howard, 1990; Strong, 1925). Our primary contributions are summarized as follows: We propose Co-Director, multi-agent architecture for video storytelling that achieves global coherence by integrating multi-armed bandit optimization with local, MLLM feedback-driven refinement loop. We introduce the hierarchical parameterization of creative intent, enabling the consistent propagation of visual and narrative styles across discrete generative sub-agents. We present GenAd-Bench to rigorously evaluate generative video storytelling across 400 scenarios, eliminating memorization bias via the use of fictional brand assets. We demonstrate our approach significantly outperforming existing foundation models and agentic baselines in asset preservation, contextual alignment, and visual quality. We open-source our implementation of Co-Director and the GenAd-Bench dataset to facilitate future research in generative video storytelling. 2. Related Work Foundation Models for Video Synthesis While large-scale diffusion models (ByteDance, 2026; Google, 2026d; Kuaishou, 2026; Wan et al., 2025) achieve unprecedented cinematic realism, maintaining long-range consistency remains challenging (Elmoghany et al., 2025). Specialized architectures 2 Co-Director : Agentic Generative Video Storytelling Figure 1 Co-Director multi-agent pipeline: (Top): The Orchestrator Agent (Φ𝑜𝑟𝑐ℎ) utilizes MAB to navigate factored creative action space 𝜃 = (𝜃𝑐𝑠, 𝜃𝑛𝑚, 𝜃𝑎𝑎). (Bottom): The Pre-Production Agent (Φ𝑝𝑟𝑒) synthesizes creative brief (B), storyline (L), and visual assets (V) to establish consistent storyboard (S), which the Production Agent (Φ𝑝𝑟𝑜𝑑) translates into synchronized keyframes (K), video clips (C), and audio (A). An MLLM Judge evaluates the final video (𝐹) to generate factored reward signal 𝑅 = (𝑟𝑐𝑠, 𝑟𝑛𝑚, 𝑟𝑎𝑎), which is propagated back to the MAB to iteratively refine the creative configuration over 𝑇 optimization loops. like Phantom (Liu et al., 2025) and MoC (Cai et al., 2025) introduce advanced techniques to maintain character consistency, but they remain fundamentally standalone generators. Furthermore, these models serve as powerful building blocks rather than complete end-to-end solutions, lacking the high-level planning and reasoning required to create complex narratives in the resulting video output (Elmoghany et al., 2025). Agentic Frameworks for Video Storytelling To bridge the gap between clip generation and narrative production, recent multi-agent systems utilize LLMs to decompose scripts into shot lists and prompts (Lin et al., 2023; Wu et al., 2025b), targeting domains like personalized vlogs (Hou et al., 2025), music videos (Tang et al., 2025), and short films (Mu et al., 2026; Wei et al., 2025; Xiao et al., 2025; Zhao et al., 2024). However, these waterfall pipelines are susceptible to cascading errors. While frameworks like AniMaker (Shi et al., 2025) and MAViS (Wang et al., 2026) incorporate local consistency checks, they struggle with global credit assignment (Minsky, 1961) across the agentic stack. To resolve this, Co-Director moves beyond the local-refinement paradigm by introducing Multi-Armed Bandit (MAB) formulation (Bubeck and Cesa-Bianchi, 2012; Lai and Robbins, 1985) to perform principled exploration-exploitation of the creative latent space. This global, hierarchical parameterization resolves the semantic collisions between sub-agents that plague current pipelines, preventing identity drift and ensuring horizontal cohesiveness. Benchmarks for Video Storytelling As generative capabilities evolve, evaluation methodologies must adapt to measure higher-order reasoning. Generic video benchmarks (Huang et al., 2024; Sun et al., 2025) focus on prompt alignment and broad aesthetics, lacking the visual and narrative qualities demanded by professional workflows. Furthermore, even recent narrative-focused benchmarks, such as MovieBench (Wu et al., 2025a) and ViStoryBench (Zhuang et al., 2025), strictly evaluate isolated generative sub-tasks using meticulously pre-defined scripts. Consequently, current literature lacks evaluations for true end-to-end storytelling: the complex process of autonomously transforming simple, abstract idea into cohesive video narrative. Our work addresses this gap by introducing GenAd-Bench designed specifically for end-to-end generative workflows. 3 Co-Director : Agentic Generative Video Storytelling 3. Approach 3.1. Hierarchical Multi-Agent Architecture We formulate Co-Director as hierarchical multi-agent system orchestrating generative video workflows. While the framework is domain-agnostic, we instantiate it to solve video advertising, which comes with strict narrative and visual constraints. Orchestrator Agent (Φ𝑜𝑟𝑐ℎ) This agent manages the global state and execution flow of all sub-agents. Before downstream generation, it performs multimodal data ingestion: 1) parsing the user prompt 𝑝𝑢𝑠𝑒𝑟 into structured constraints ˆ𝑝𝑢𝑠𝑒𝑟 (brand, product, and demographics (Baardman et al., 2021)); 2) annotating reference visuals I𝑟𝑒 𝑓 with captions and semantic roles (e.g., product, logo, protagonist), yielding ˆI𝑟𝑒 𝑓 . Post-initialization, Φ𝑜𝑟𝑐ℎ drives global optimization loop for 𝑇 iterations. At iteration 𝑡, it interfaces with multi-armed bandit (Section 3.3) to sample creative configuration 𝜃(𝑡) , forming global conditioning context Θ (𝑡) = ( ˆ𝑝𝑢𝑠𝑒𝑟, ˆI𝑟𝑒 𝑓 , 𝜃(𝑡) ),1 which is then propagated downstream. Pre-Production Agent (Φ𝑝𝑟𝑒) This agent transforms the structured constraints Θ into descriptive storyboard through four sequential modules: Creative Brief (B): Acting as the deep research phase, this module performs contextual enrichment, expanding product/demographic constraints within ˆ𝑝𝑢𝑠𝑒𝑟 into localized cultural resonance and environmental context. This becomes creative brief B. Storyline (L): Conditioned on and 𝜃, this module constructs the storyline that acts as scene-by-scene script establishing plot, character actions, pacing, and transitions. Visual Assets (V): This module generates visual assets for entities in (e.g., characters, props, environments) only if they are absent from the reference visuals ˆI𝑟𝑒 𝑓 Θ. For characters, it synthesizes context-aligned wardrobes dictated by L, compositing the base character and garments into multi-angle reference collages. Storyboard (S): Expands into storyboard = {𝑠0, . . . , 𝑠𝑁 1} modulated by 𝜃. Each scene 𝑠𝑖 defines semantic descriptors (actions, environment), camera kinematics (scale, movements), temporal constraints, and entity presence flags linked to V. It also formulates global audio directives (voiceover, tempo, mood), inspired by consumer psychology literature (Alpert and Alpert, 1990; Gorn, 1982). Production Agent (Φ𝑝𝑟𝑜𝑑) This agent translates the storyboard into multimodal artifacts via three specialized sub-agents: Keyframe Agent (Φ 𝑓 𝑟𝑎𝑚𝑒): Direct text-to-video generation is prone to identity drift (Elmoghany et al., 2025). To mitigate this, Φ 𝑓 𝑟𝑎𝑚𝑒 establishes visual prior by generating first-frame images to condition downstream synthesis, modulated by aesthetic constraints in 𝜃 (Section 3.3). Formally, for each scene, it processes descriptors 𝑠𝑖, assigned assets V, and configuration 𝜃 to output the keyframe set K: = {𝑘0, 𝑘1, . . . , 𝑘𝑁 1} where 𝑘𝑖 = Φ 𝑓 𝑟𝑎𝑚𝑒(𝑠𝑖, V, 𝜃) (1) Video Agent (Φ𝑣𝑖𝑑𝑒𝑜): This sub-agent transforms into sequence of video clips C. For each scene, it conditions video diffusion model on the initial keyframe 𝑘𝑖, parameterized by local descriptors 𝑠𝑖 and creative configuration 𝜃 to dictate temporal dynamics like pacing and camera kinematics (Section 3.3). Formally, this yields the clip set C: 1We omit superscript (𝑡) hereafter for clarity. = {𝑐0, 𝑐1, . . . , 𝑐𝑁 1} where 𝑐𝑖 = Φ𝑣𝑖𝑑𝑒𝑜(𝑠𝑖, 𝑘𝑖, 𝜃) (2) 4 Co-Director : Agentic Generative Video Storytelling Audio Agent (Φ𝑎𝑢𝑑𝑖𝑜): This sub-agent synthesizes the acoustic elements specified in S. Rather than per-scene generation, it processes global voiceover and background music from the planning phase. Acoustic properties (e.g., tempo, genre, mood) are conditioned on 𝜃. Φ𝑎𝑢𝑑𝑖𝑜 employs text-to-speech and text-to-audio models to generate and mix these discrete elements into unified global track A. Formally: = Φ𝑎𝑢𝑑𝑖𝑜(S, 𝜃) (3) Post-Production Agent (Φ𝑝𝑜𝑠𝑡) This module consolidates clips and audio into the final video 𝐹. It concatenates based on temporal constraints defined in storyboard S, then multiplexes onto the sequence to synchronize visual pacing with acoustic cues. 𝐹 = Φ𝑝𝑜𝑠𝑡 (S, C, A) (4) 3.2. Local Optimization via Agentic Self-Refinement In sequential generative pipelines, early errors propagate and amplify downstream. To prevent this, Co-Director implements feedback descent (Lee et al., 2025; Yuksekgonul et al., 2024) to self-refine intermediate artifacts. LLMs and MLLMs iteratively evaluate and refine artifacts based on textual feedback. This loop terminates upon reaching score threshold or iteration budget, returning the best attempt. We apply this local optimization to storyline and keyframes K, reserving final video evaluation for global reward signal (Section 3.3) Storyline Before Φ𝑝𝑟𝑒 generates storyboard S, an LLM assesses storyline on hook quality, narrative cohesion, product integration, emotional engagement, and prompt adherence. It outputs score with textual feedback and rewrites the prompt to emphasize areas needing improvement. If the score falls below threshold 𝜏L, the agent regenerates using this revised prompt, ensuring coherent narrative foundation before expansion into S. Keyframe An MLLM evaluates the keyframe sequence = {𝑘0, . . . , 𝑘𝑁 1} jointly rather than individually. This joint contextualization evaluates sequence-level consistency for character and product identities, alongside environmental continuity. Comparing against ˆ𝑝𝑢𝑠𝑒𝑟 and V, it scores visual consistency, narrative flow, product appeal, and prompt adherence. If the aggregate score falls below threshold 𝜏K, the MLLM isolates issues and indexes problematic keyframes in K. It then provides actionable feedback and refined prompts, allowing Φ 𝑓 𝑟𝑎𝑚𝑒 to selectively regenerate flagged keyframes while preserving accepted ones. 3.3. Global Optimization via Multi-Armed Bandits Because the chained pipeline is non-differentiable, standard gradient updates cannot achieve end-toend optimization. Instead, Co-Director uses hierarchical parameterization, treating the pipeline as black box steered by centralized parameters governing global creative directions. We formulate tuning these parameters as Multi-Armed Bandit (MAB) problem (Bubeck and Cesa-Bianchi, 2012; Lai and Robbins, 1985). Operating at this top level, rather than tuning individual prompts independently, ensures semantic coherence across sub-agents while balancing the exploration of novel strategies with the exploitation of proven configurations. In standard MAB framework, an agent iteratively selects an action (an arm), MAB Preliminaries observes reward, and updates its policy to maximize long-term payout. The central challenge is the exploration-exploitation tradeoff : balancing testing untried arms for potentially higher rewards against exploiting the best-known option. In Co-Director, single pull corresponds to full pipeline execution, the arms are creative directions 𝜃, and the reward is the terminal video evaluation score. 5 Co-Director : Agentic Generative Video Storytelling Hierarchical Parameterization We define the action space (the arms) over three orthogonal axes of video production grounded in marketing and media theory. The creative direction 𝜃 is tuple comprising three categorical variables: 𝜃 = (𝜃𝑐𝑠, 𝜃𝑛𝑚, 𝜃𝑎𝑎). Creative Strategy (𝜃𝑐𝑠): This controls what the ad is fundamentally trying to communicate (Laskey et al., 1989). The bandit selects among an Informational strategy (focusing on functional utility), Transformational strategy (driving psychological and lifestyle appeal), or Comparative strategy (positioning against known standard or competitor). Narrative Mode (𝜃𝑛𝑚): This controls how the strategy is delivered over time (Escalas, 2004). The bandit selects among an Analytical mode (an argument-based delivery without story arc), Vignette mode (atmospheric slices-of-life prioritizing mood over plot), or Narrative Drama mode (a character-driven sequence of conflict and resolution). Aesthetic Archetype (𝜃𝑎𝑎): This controls the sensory realization of the video (Zettl, 1973) via four configurations: Clarity/Energy (high-key lighting, fast-cut motion, high-tempo audio); Cinematic Premium (chiaroscuro lighting, slow reflective camera work, orchestral audio); Minimalist Focus (bright backgrounds, micro-movements, ASMR-style foley); and Kinetic Grit (low-key lighting, handheld/FPV drone motion, electronic synth audio). At execution step 𝑡, the MAB selects an arm combination 𝜃(𝑡) . While downstream LLMs can interpret these creative parameters, independent interpretation risks semantic drift. To ensure coherence, orchestrator Φ𝑜𝑟𝑐ℎ centrally synthesizes 𝜃(𝑡) into agent-specific instructions, dynamically injecting them into placeholders within the agents system prompts. Reward To evaluate 𝜃(𝑡) , Co-Director uses an MLLM evaluator processing the final video 𝐹 (𝑡) alongside 𝑝𝑢𝑠𝑒𝑟 and I𝑟𝑒 𝑓 to yield two metrics (full prompt in Appendix K). First, an aggregate quality score (temporal coherence, visual quality, consistency) selects the best video across 𝑇 executions. Second, it computes dimension-specific strategic efficacy scores crucial for MAB optimization. This decouples execution from strategy, assessing if the creative direction was effective independent of downstream diffusion artifacts. This yields factored reward signal 𝑅 (𝑡) = (𝑟𝑐𝑠, 𝑟𝑛𝑚, 𝑟𝑎𝑎) [0, 100]3 to update the bandits policy. Optimization Policy and Warm Start Co-Director models the search using the Upper Confidence Bound (UCB1) algorithm (Auer et al., 2002), which uses the disentangled scalars from 𝑅 (𝑡) to independently update the expected value of each selected arm. By decoupling the reward updates, the MAB analytically bypasses the credit assignment problem (Minsky, 1961), isolating exactly which creative strategies drove the videos success or failure. Furthermore, because unguided exploration is prohibitively expensive in video generation, we introduce an LLM-driven warm start. Prior to the first execution, an LLM analyzes the users demographic and product domain to initialize the expected values of the arms, safely biasing early exploration toward theoretically sound configurations. 4. GenAd-Bench: Evaluating Generative Video Advertising To construct GenAd-Bench, we developed human-in-the-loop pipeline using Gemini 3 Pro (Google, 2026b) for text synthesis and Nano Banana Pro (Google, 2026c) for visual assets. We generated 50 fictional brands with four products each, spanning consumer goods to industrial equipment. For each product, we authored two contrasting demographicsstereotypical and unconventionalyielding 400 unique scenarios defined by six-point prompt (brand, product, gender, age, location, interest). Accompanying visual assets (logos, product references) were generated and manually verified (15% required regeneration) to ensure uniqueness and prevent visual memorization or copyright conflicts. The resulting benchmark enforces strict targeting constraints and multimodal conditioning, 6 Co-Director : Agentic Generative Video Storytelling requiring models to synthesize textual demographic intent with specific reference imagery. By evaluating paired scenarios across global geographic distribution, GenAd-Bench explicitly tests conceptual flexibility and exposure to ingrained biases. Furthermore, by utilizing fictional entities, the benchmark prevents models from defaulting to memorized training priors, ensuring that performance is measure of reasoning rather than retrieval. We partition the data into half (200 scenarios each) for hillclimbing and validation. We use Gemini 3 Pro (Google, 2026b) as an MLLM-as-a-Judge to evaluate generated videos; see Appendix for the rubric prompts. The MLLM simultaneously processes visual frames and the audio track to score four dimensions (0100): Visual Asset Fidelity (VAF): Measures faithfulness to user-provided logos and products. The MLLM assesses visual-semantic similarity, ensuring core identity preservation across complex camera angles, occlusions, and dynamic lighting. Demographic Alignment (DA): Assesses targeting success across four variables (Gender, Age, Location, Interest). The MLLM penalizes generic aesthetics while rewarding tailored casting, localized environmental context, and narrative tone. Marketing Appeal (MA): Evaluates advertisement persuasiveness via the AIDA hierarchy (Barry and Howard, 1990; Strong, 1925) and engagement theory (Teixeira et al., 2012). It scores visual hooks, value proposition clarity, and emotional resonance. Visual Quality (VQ): prompt-agnostic metric scanning for diffusion artifacts, object morphing, and temporal flickering. It ensures videos meet broadcast viability by penalizing unnatural motion or violations of basic physical laws. While utilizing an MLLM for evaluation raises potential bias concerns, we benchmark these metrics against human study (Section 5.2). We find that the MLLM serves as reliable proxy, showing alignment that approaches the human-human agreement ceiling. 5. Experiments We evaluate Co-Director on GenAd-Bench against state-of-the-art baselines, demonstrating its superior storytelling capabilities, followed by ablation studies validating the necessity of local self-refinement and global MAB optimization. Implementation Details We instantiate Co-Director with Gemini 3 Pro (Google, 2026b), Nano Banana Pro (Google, 2026c), Veo 3.1 (Google, 2026d), Gemini 2.5 Pro TTS (Google, 2026a), and Lyria 2 (Google, 2025). MAB optimization is set to 𝑇 = 4 iterations (see App. for search efficiency trade-offs), and local refinement is capped at 3 retries. Final videos feature 4 shots totaling 12 seconds. Baselines We evaluate Co-Director against monolithic models, commercial platforms, and multiagent frameworks, listed in Table 1. To isolate architectural efficacy, we compare against analogous agentic pipelines AniMaker and MovieAgent using the same underlying models. Since these require scene-by-scene scripts, Gemini 3 Pro expands our six-point prompts into the necessary formats. Finally, we compare Co-Director against Base Agentic Pipeline (no optimizations) and Random Search Baseline (𝑇 = 4) that samples the creative space without MAB or warm-up. Baseline details are in Appendix E. 7 Co-Director : Agentic Generative Video Storytelling Table 1 Evaluation on GenAd-Bench. All metrics are scaled [0, 100]. VAF: Visual Asset Fidelity, DA: Demographic Alignment, MA: Marketing Appeal, VQ: Visual Quality.

Main Results As reported in Table 1, Co-Director achieves the highest average performance (81.4). We include specialized commercial platforms (Creatify, HeyGen) to establish the current industry baseline; their reliance on "talking head" avatar synthesis with static overlays fundamentally limits their performance on complex narratives, highlighting the necessity of Co-Directors dynamic storytelling. Crucially, the performance gap between Co-Director and prior agentic baselines (AniMaker, MovieAgent) stems strictly from agentic architectural differences. By utilizing the same LLM (Gemini 3 Pro) to generate their requisite input scripts, we ensured highly competitive and equitable starting point. Furthermore, self-refinement (Section 3.2) and MAB optimization (Section 3.3) drive Co-Directors superior Visual Quality (VQ: 70.2) over its base model, Veo 3.1 (VQ: 50.5). Although VQ is promptagnostic, Co-Directors optimization proactively improves suboptimal clips, effectively elevating the foundation models baseline and ensuring superior temporal consistency and physical realism over raw, single-pass generation. To isolate the impact of our orchestration and global optimization, we evaluate Co-Director against both external baselines and internal architectural variants. Using identical foundation models, our Base Agentic Pipeline (𝑇 = 1) averages 68.5. This proves our core orchestration alone significantly outperforms prior linear open-source pipelines like AniMaker (62.2) and MovieAgent (65.3), even before iterative refinement. Scaling to 𝑇 = 4 via Random Search (75.7), akin to Best-of-N (Snell et al., 2024), yields noticeable improvements. Crucially, replacing random sampling with our MAB formulation and warm-up (Co-Director, 𝑇 = 4) drives the average to 81.4, demonstrating that our ultimate performance gains stem from combination of superior reasoning, planning, and principled global optimization. As shown in Figure 2, comparing cumulative best performance reveals that Co-Director Warm-Up + MAB provides an efficiency advantage, achieving high-quality results in the first iteration where Random Search fails to ground the creative intent. While Random Search shows steadier upward trajectory over long horizons, this reflects the variance of undirected sampling; it eventually "discovers" strong configurations by chance, whereas the MAB rapidly isolates an optimal plateau. This suggests the system effectively converges on strategically sound local optimum early, avoiding the prohibitive computational costs of the exhaustive search required for Random Search to reach Co-Director : Agentic Generative Video Storytelling Figure 2 MAB Optimization Efficiency. Cumulative best performance over 10 iterations comparing Co-Directors MAB optimization against an undirected Random Search. comparable parity. 5.2. MLLM Metric Validation via Human Evaluation To validate the MLLM-as-a-Judge metrics, we conducted human study on 50-scenario subset. Each video was evaluated by 5 independent raters along the four dimensions (VAF, DA, MA, VQ) on 5-point Likert scale. Table 2 reports Krippendorffs Alpha (𝛼), Cohens Kappa (𝜅), Pearson (𝑟), Spearman (𝜌), and Mean Absolute Error (MAE), establishing human agreement as the theoretical upper bound. The MLLM demonstrates strong alignment, approaching this ceiling in complex dimensions like DA and MA. Visual Quality (VQ) exhibits lower correlation due to differing evaluation mechanics: humans viewing real-time playback often find transient diffusion artifacts less visually disruptive. The MLLM, conversely, processes frames analytically, strictly penalizing brief structural or temporal inconsistencies. Thus, while serving as highly aligned proxy for narrative evaluation, the MLLMs VQ score acts as strict physical quality gate rather than direct mirror of subjective human perception. Table 2 Human agreement and correlation statistics across 50 scenarios. Human-human agreement serves as the theoretical ceiling for MLLM-human alignment.

Ablation Studies MAB Logic We compare our MAB search logic (1) against two variants. Reverting to cold start (2) degrades performance, as lacking strong initialization produces higher-variance trajectories during early exploration. Crucially, collapsing the multi-dimensional arm-specific reward into single scalar (3)using the holistic score meant for final video selectionreduces VAF by 5.0 points. This validates our factored reward design. By leveraging the MLLM evaluator to decouple the overall video score from arm-specific rewards, the MAB isolates the exact parameters driving success (see App. K). Self-Refinement Loops Removing Storyline Refinement (4) primarily penalizes semantic metrics like MA and DA, validating its impact on narrative coherence. Removing Keyframe Refinement (5) causes steep 9.8-point VAF drop, confirming its vital role in preserving product visual identity. Stripping all local refinement and global optimization yields barebones single-pass baseline (8) (67.2 Avg.). Our complete architecture outperforms this linear performance floor by relative 17.6%. 5.4. Qualitative Results and Discussion To complement our quantitative analysis, Appendix provides qualitative results showcasing generation artifacts across GenAd-Bench scenarios. These examples demonstrate Co-Directors ability to interpret abstract ideas into cohesive visual narratives with precise scene instructions. Our observations highlight three primary strengths: (1) Strategic Adaptability: evoking expected atmospheres for stereotypical prompts while maintaining consistency in unconventional contexts; (2) Identity Preservation: achieving character and product consistency across dynamic poses, varied angles, and dramatic environmental transitions; and (3) Logical Robustness: local self-refinement correcting LLM hallucinations like misidentified product categories (App. H). Furthermore, evaluation on ViStoryBench-Lite (Zhuang et al., 2025) (App. C) confirms Co-Directors core consistency and creative steering generalize to non-advertising cinematic narratives, outperforming specialized base10 Co-Director : Agentic Generative Video Storytelling lines in style and character fidelity. We encourage readers to consult the appendix and supplementary videos for complete trace of pipeline reasoning and rendered outputs. 6. Conclusion We present Co-Director, hierarchical multi-agent framework that formulates video storytelling as global optimization problem. By navigating creative latent space via MAB-driven steering, our system achieves high-fidelity narrative consistency that traditionally requires large-scale production teams. Furthermore, to evaluate this capability, we introduce GenAd-Bench, rigorous dataset designed to assess end-to-end generative workflows under strict targeting and visual constraints. Experimental results confirm that Co-Director outperforms both monolithic models and existing agentic pipelines, offering principled, highly efficient approach to autonomous visual storytelling that generalizes beyond advertising to broader cinematic narratives.

Appendix Overview To assist in navigating the supplemental materials, this section provides directory of the contents contained within this appendix. Each section covers distinct component of our results, dataset, or methodology: Section B: Qualitative Results on GenAd-Bench Visual galleries demonstrating CoDirectors ability to preserve brand identity across diverse narrative arcs. Section C: Evaluation on General Video Storytelling Performance metrics and qualitative comparisons on the ViStoryBench benchmark (Zhuang et al., 2025). Section D: GenAd-Bench Details Details on dataset construction and descriptive statistics. Section E: Baseline Implementation Details Standardized configurations, prompt templates, and foundation models used for all evaluated baselines. Section F: MAB Optimization Efficiency Analysis of sample efficiency and the convergence of our multi-armed bandit creative steering. Section G: Creative Direction Examples Walkthroughs of hierarchical parameterization and how aesthetic archetypes influence visual output. Section H: Local Optimization via Self-Refinement Evidence of the agentic loops ability to correct structural hallucinations and identity drift. Section I: Storyline Evaluation Prompt The system prompt and logic used to evaluate narrative coherence in storyline. Section J: Keyframe Evaluation Prompt The system prompt used to assess visual consistency and character preservation in keyframes. Section K: Final Video Evaluation Prompt The system prompt and rubric for scoring end-to-end video quality and creative alignment. Section L: GenAd-Bench Evaluation System Prompts The complete set of automated scoring rubrics for the proposed benchmark. B. Qualitative Results on GenAd-Bench This section presents qualitative results that demonstrate Co-Directors ability to translate six-point product prompts and reference visuals (brand logos and products) into cohesive and high-fidelity visual narratives. 14 Co-Director : Agentic Generative Video Storytelling Figure 3 shows synthesized storylines alongside their corresponding keyframes across four distinct scenarios. These examples highlight the frameworks ability to ground abstract marketing conceptssuch as functional bellows effect or psychological shift toward safetyinto precise scene instructions with rich emotional arcs. Furthermore, they showcase the models robust preservation of character and product identity across dynamic physical poses, varied camera angles, and dramatic environmental transitions. To further illustrate the pipelines versatility, Figure 4 provides keyframe sequences from 10 additional scenarios, spanning both stereotypical and unconventional product contexts. Co-Director consistently evokes the appropriate atmosphere for expected settings, such as rustic or softly lit environments, while maintaining structural and temporal consistency in unconventional scenarios. This includes displaying the correct object manipulation in unexpected spaces (e.g., high-tech workspace or library) and handling complex lighting conditions like neon urban streets. C. Evaluation on General Video Storytelling In the main paper, we chose video advertising as our main demonstration scenario because it highlights novel aspect of our framework: the ability to generate full video narrative from single, highlevel idea, rather than needing detailed, scene-by-scene script. Existing agentic systems, such as AniMaker (Shi et al., 2025) and MovieAgent (Wu et al., 2025b), require structured, scene-byscene scripts provided priori. In contrast, our framework synthesizes full cohesive narratives from brief tagline (e.g., our six-point prompt). While MAViS (Wang et al., 2026) attempts similar idea-to-video pipeline, their evaluation relies on closed set of 10 custom prompts, precluding reproducible comparison. This underscores the critical importance of our proposed GenAd-Bench. Existing visual narrative datasets, such as MovieBench (Wu et al., 2025a) and ViStoryBench (Zhuang et al., 2025), evaluate isolated generative steps (e.g., text-to-image or image-to-video synthesis) rather than the complete video storytelling pipeline. GenAd-Bench fills this void by providing rigorous, reproducible standard for evaluating the entire trajectory of video storytellingfrom abstract conceptualization to final rendered output. Despite the architectural mismatch between our end-to-end framework and traditional staged benchmarks, we evaluate Co-Director on ViStoryBench-Lite (Zhuang et al., 2025) to demonstrate its generality across domains. Because ViStoryBench evaluates storyboard generationproviding strict shot-by-shot scripts and character reference images as inputs, and expecting static image outputs rather than videowe ablate our pipeline accordingly. Specifically, we bypass our script generation and video synthesis modules, isolating and deploying only the Keyframe Agent, the local self-refinement loop, and the global MAB-steered optimization. This allows us to prove that Co-Directors core consistency and creative direction mechanisms remain highly effective even when constrained to rigid, pre-defined narrative structures. To ensure rigorous evaluation, we deliberately select agentic video generation pipelines from the official ViStoryBench leaderboard as our baselines, as their modular architectures provide the most direct and equitable comparison to our own. As shown in Table 5, Co-Director achieves strong performance in visual consistency, securing the highest scores in Style (Cross: 0.499, Self: 0.743) and Character (Cross: 0.499, Self: 0.593) consistency metrics. Furthermore, it yields the highest average Prompt Alignment (3.166), demonstrating superior control over camera dynamics and local context compared to prior agentic baselines like AniMaker and MovieAgent. While Co-Director trails in Quality & Diversity (e.g., Inception Score: 10.900) and On-stage Character Count Matching (OCCM: 66.215), these metrics fundamentally conflict with cohesive storytelling. Inception Score favors high visual variance, naturally opposing our models strict cross-scene consistency. Likewise, OCCM enforces rigid closed-world character 15 Co-Director : Agentic Generative Video Storytelling Figure 3 Qualitative results on GenAd-Bench. Co-Director translates complex product prompts into cohesive visual narratives. The framework maintains character and product identity across dynamic poses and varied angles (top) and preserves precise product details during dramatic environmental and lighting transitions (bottom). 16 Co-Director : Agentic Generative Video Storytelling Figure 4 Additional qualitative results on GenAd-Bench. Co-Director effectively generates diverse visual narratives across both stereotypical and unconventional product scenarios. It successfully evokes expected atmospheres for stereotypical prompts, such as the rustic warmth of FredBaz-Syrup or the gentle nursery lighting of Xyzzy-Bear. The pipeline shows robustness to unconventional contexts, maintaining consistency in character/product identity and complex lighting (e.g., neon urban streets in EveFoo-Helmet) while executing precise object manipulation in unexpected environments like library (LoremBaz-Pillow) or high-tech workspace (Wandom-Glue). 17 Co-Director : Agentic Generative Video Storytelling count, naturally penalizing Co-Director for organically generating unprompted background actors (e.g., pedestrians in bustling city) that enhance cinematic realism. These quantitative metrics are directly corroborated by qualitative observations. As illustrated in Figure 5, Co-Director reliably generates temporally coherent frame sequences across diverse narratives, successfully preserving both character identity and background spatial structures over time. closer examination of specific sequence (Figure 6) further highlights the frameworks strong prompt alignment and character preservation. While minor anatomical artifacts, such as occasional left-right hand confusion, can still manifest as an artifact of the underlying diffusion process, the frameworks overall visual fidelity and narrative continuity remain exceptionally high. Ultimately, these results illustrate that Co-Director is not narrowly overfitted to marketing content, but is instead highly capable engine for generalized visual storytelling. Crucially, video advertising should not be viewed as restrictive niche; rather, it is demanding subset of general storytelling characterized by rigid, testable constraints on brand fidelity, demographic alignment, and runtime. By mastering these rigorous constraints, Co-Director demonstrates foundational narrative competency that seamlessly transfers to broader cinematic and storytelling domains. D. GenAd-Bench Details To rigorously evaluate video storytelling, we introduce GenAd-Bench. This appendix details the datasets construction process, comprehensive demographic and category statistics, our data split strategy, and qualitative examples. D.1. Dataset Construction Pipeline Current video evaluation benchmarks predominantly rely on generic, short-horizon prompts (e.g., dog running on grass) and evaluate well-known entities, allowing models to rely heavily on parametric memory. GenAd-Bench circumvents this by utilizing human-in-the-loop pipeline to synthesize 200 fictitious products across 50 fictitious brands. We utilized Gemini 3 Pro Google (2026b) to generate the structural text metadata and Nano Banana Pro (Google, 2026c) to generate the visual reference assets. The construction followed hierarchical schema: 1. Brand & Product Synthesis: The LLM generated 50 unique brands and assigned exactly 4 distinct products to each. Products were explicitly prompted to span wide range of form factors, including small consumer goods, large industrial equipment, and intangible offerings (e.g., software and subscription services). 2. Paired Scenario Generation: For each of the 200 products, the LLM generated two contrasting Table 5 Evaluation on ViStoryBench-Lite. The generated sequence demonstrates strong prompt alignment and robust character consistency across frames, though minor anatomical artifacts (e.g., left-right hand confusion) occasionally occur. 20 Co-Director : Agentic Generative Video Storytelling Figure 7 GenAd-Bench reference visuals. The reference set comprises brand logos and product images, with each brand featuring four product pairs specifically curated to represent both stereotypical and unconventional target demographics. Co-Director : Agentic Generative Video Storytelling target demographics to test conceptual flexibility: Stereotypical scenario (aligned with traditional marketing tropes) and an Unconventional scenario (subverting traditional expectations). 3. Visual Asset Generation: The text-to-image model generated distinct vector-style logo for each of the 50 brands. Subsequently, it generated product reference images conditioned on both the textual product description and the respective brand logo to ensure visual-semantic integration. 4. Manual Verification & Quality Control: To ensure high-fidelity inputs for the product reference generation stage, we first manually verified and corrected all brand logos. Following the generation of these product reference visuals, approximately 15% were regenerated to address improper logo integration, logical inconsistencies, or unintended visual memorization (e.g., designs too closely resembling products existing in the real-world). This targeted refinement ensures the uniqueness and brand accuracy of the primary visual assets before they are propagated downstream. D.2. Taxonomy and Distribution Statistics GenAd-Bench contains 400 evaluation scenarios distributed across diverse range of industries and global contexts. Macro-Category Distribution The dataset challenges models to render various physical properties, including reflective metals (Jewelry), transparent liquids (Perfumes), and complex dynamics (Drones, Apparel). As illustrated in Figure 8a, the 200 products span 24 distinct macro-categories. High-frequency categories such as Home Goods (24), Food (20), Fashion (20), and Tech (20), test foundational object generation, while specialized domains like Automotive (8), Logistics (4), and Baby (4) probe the models ability to synthesize niche, domain-specific contexts without hallucination. Demographic and Geographic Parity To ensure the multi-armed bandit optimization does not merely exploit ingrained training biases, GenAd-Bench explicitly enforces balanced demographic and geographic distributions. As shown in Figure 8d, the dataset maintains exact gender parity: 50% (200) of the target personas are Male, and 50% (200) are Female. Crucially, because paired scenarios invert the demographic targets for the exact same product, gender bias is neutralized at the product level. Target age brackets (Figure 8b) uniformly represent four key life stages: Gen (1824), Millennials (2534), Gen (3550), and Seniors (50+). Finally, the framework demands robust cultural and environmental alignment beyond traditional Western-centric backdrops; the scenarios are grounded in 183 uniquely prompted global locations. While the dataset spans all six major continents, it deliberately concentrates on the two most diverse global consumer markets: Europe (158 scenarios) and Asia (108 scenarios). Within these clusters, geographic representation remains highly granular. The European scenarios span from primary Western hubs (e.g., France, UK, Germany) to Nordic and Eastern European locales, while the Asian distribution covers major East Asian centers (Japan, China, South Korea) alongside robust representation across Southeast Asia, India, and the Middle East. D.3. Dataset Split We partitioned the 400-scenario dataset into two equal 200-scenario splits. The Hillclimbing Set is reserved strictly iterative system optimization and is excluded from the evaluations presented in this work. All performance metrics are reported solely on the Validation Set, which is stratified into two sub-challenges to assess Co-Directors generalization capabilities across 45 unique brands: In-Domain (80% of Val / 160 Scenarios): Contains 80 products from 40 brands that share macro-categories with the Hillclimbing set. This evaluates systems ability to extrapolate optimized strategies to entirely novel products and brands within familiar industries. 22 Co-Director : Agentic Generative Video Storytelling Figure 8 Dataset Statistics for GenAd-Bench. The benchmark enforces rigorous categorical, demographic, and geographic diversity to prevent generative collapse. (a) The macro-category distribution spans wide array of physical domains. (b) Target age demographics cover four distinct life stages. (c) Scenarios are situated across 183 unique global locations, grouped here into six continental macro-regions. (d) Target gender distributions are perfectly balanced at the product level to neutralize latent generation biases. Out-of-Domain (20% of Val / 40 Scenarios): Contains 20 products from 5 brands drawn from distinct industries (e.g., Pets, Jewelry) that are absent from the Hillclimbing set. This tests systems broader generalization capabilities when handling product categories for which no category-specific optimization has occurred. E. Baseline Implementation Details Monolithic and Commercial Platforms To evaluate the monolithic foundation models and specialized commercial platformsLTX-2.3 (HaCohen et al., 2024), Kling 3.0 Omni (Kuaishou, 2026), Veo 3.1 (Google, 2026d), Wan 2.6 (Wan et al., 2025), Creatify (Creatify AI, 2026), and HeyGen (HeyGen, 2026)we employ standardized zero-shot prompting strategy. Each system is queried using the template: Generate video advertisement. [SIX-POINT PROMPT]. Reserve the last 1 second for an ending scene highlighting the provided brand logo image. This ensures uniform evaluation setting that tests each baselines ability to interpret the core product brief and integrate branding constraints. All the baseline methods natively generate audio given text prompt. 23 Co-Director : Agentic Generative Video Storytelling Multi-Agent Frameworks We compare against two structurally analogous agentic pipelines, AniMaker (Shi et al., 2025) and MovieAgent (Wu et al., 2025b), to isolate the architectural efficacy of our multi-agent system from the underlying generative backbones. Because these baselines require structured, multi-shot scripts rather than abstract product briefs, we introduced Script Generator module. This module leverages Gemini 3 Pro (Google, 2026b) to decompose the initial prompt into structured JSON script. The resulting script explicitly dictates the narrative arc, scene-specific visual prompts, voice-over text, and precise shot durations, enforced by strict 12-second total video length constraint. To maintain controlled comparison against our framework, these baselines are instantiated using identical foundation models: Veo 3.1 (Google, 2026d) for video generation and Gemini 2.5 Pro TTS (Google, 2026a) for voiceovers F. Extended Discussion on MAB Optimization Efficiency While standard multi-armed bandit (MAB) formulations often analyze asymptotic convergence over thousands of iterations, video generation imposes extreme computational and financial constraints. An exhaustive grid search of our defined creative action space (𝜃𝑐𝑠 𝜃𝑛𝑚 𝜃𝑎𝑎) would require 36 discrete, end-to-end video generation pipelines per prompt. While this brute-force approach guarantees finding the global optimum, it renders the system entirely unscalable and defeats the purpose of an accessible agentic framework. Therefore, the goal of our MAB formulation is not infinite-horizon exploration, but rather sample efficiency under resource constraints. We achieve this through two mechanisms: 1. Factored Rewards: By utilizing factored reward signal 𝑅 = (𝑟𝑐𝑠, 𝑟𝑛𝑚, 𝑟𝑎𝑎), single execution step 𝑡 simultaneously updates the expected values across all three independent creative axes. The system is not searching 36 distinct paths, but rather evaluating 10 independent parameters. 2. LLM-Driven Warm Start: We initialize the expected values using an LLM, safely biasing early exploration toward theoretically sound configurations and pruning the vast majority of sub-optimal combinations before iteration even begins. Consequently, as demonstrated in our empirical results  (Fig. 2)  , an iteration budget of 𝑇 = 4 is sufficient for the MAB to rapidly converge on highly effective creative configuration. This approach clearly outperforms Random Search, capturing the benefits of global search without incurring the computational overhead of exhaustive sampling. G. Creative Direction Examples G.1. Horizontal Directing through Hierarchical Parameterization Co-Director achieves global coherence through horizontal directingthe process of synthesizing disparate creative parameters into unified, high-level vision. Rather than passing raw categorical arms as static strings, the Orchestrator Agent (Φ𝑜𝑟𝑐ℎ) generates top-down creative direction that is decomposed into agent-specific technical directives. These directives are then routed to their respective sub-agents via dynamic prompt injection; specifically, the synthesized creative direction is injected into the system prompt of each agent. This ensures that the Storyline, Keyframe, and Video instructions are orthogonally aligned toward single strategic goal, preventing semantic drift by grounding each agents execution in centralized creative intent. Table 6 illustrates an example. In Iteration 1, the bandit selects an Informational strategy (𝜃𝑐𝑠) delivered via Vignette narrative mode (𝜃𝑛𝑚) and Clarity/Energy (𝜃𝑎𝑎) aesthetics. This results in Co-Director : Agentic Generative Video Storytelling directives that prioritize "functional utility" and "vibrant suburban transit," utilizing high-temporal motion and fast cuts to emphasize the efficiency of the vehicles sliding doors and safety frame within an Osaka carpool context. In contrast, in Iteration 2, the bandit pivots to Transformational strategy (𝜃𝑐𝑠) using an Analytical mode (𝜃𝑛𝑚) and Cinematic Premium (𝜃𝑎𝑎). The instructions are immediately overridden to focus on "logical manifesto" of access and protection, delivered through "Premium Noir" aesthetic. This configuration utilizes Chiaroscuro lighting and low-temporal, reflective cinematography to connect the vehicles technical specifications to the users social identity and psychological state of "unshakable composure." This horizontal consistency across different narrative and sensory modalities demonstrates that Co-Director is capable of high-level creative reasoning, ensuring that the "what," "how," and "feel" of the advertisement remain in strict strategic alignment. G.2. Visual Realization via Aesthetic Archetypes To evaluate the qualitative impact of our hierarchical parameterization, we analyze the visual output of two scenarios across four distinct Aesthetic Archetypes (𝜃𝑎𝑎). As shown in Figure 9, the MABselected configuration acts as "Digital Director," re-shooting the same product prompt to meet specific strategic intents: Clarity/Energy (the "Pop" Look): This maximizes immediate product recognition. By flooding the scene with high-key, low-contrast illumination, the system eliminates shadows and emphasizes vibrancy. In the Grault-Heel example, this manifests as clean, energetic neons; in the Con-Kibble case, it produces clinical, trustworthy brightness. Cinematic Premium (the "Movie" Look): This targets perceived prestige via chiaroscuro (highcontrast) lighting. By sculpting depth through dramatic light and shadow, the product is elevated to "hero" status. Note the sophisticated golden-hour rim lighting on the footwear and the moody, atmospheric shadows in the K9 training station. Minimalist Focus (the "Gallery" Look): Here, the system prioritizes material essentialism. By stripping away environmental noise and utilizing macro-framing against pure backgrounds, the viewer is forced to engage with the physical "truth" of the objectsuch as the intricate crystal weave of heel or the nutritional texture of kibble pellet. Kinetic Grit (the "Raw" Look): This emphasizes modern authenticity over polished perfection. Through low-key lighting and unstable handheld or FPV drone motion cues, the system captures sense of "verité" intensity. This is visible in the gritty, urban shadows of the Ibiza nightlife and the tactical, unpolished urgency of the professional K9 unit. Crucially, despite these shifts in aesthetic "vibe," the underlying reference visual assets remain consistent. The brand logos and structural features of the assets are preserved across every narrative arc, proving that our agentic steering does not compromise brand integrity. H. Local Optimization via Agentic Self-Refinement Building on the foundations of feedback descent (Lee et al., 2025; Yuksekgonul et al., 2024), we implement an agentic self-refinement loop to ensure the logical and visual integrity of intermediate artifacts. While Section 3.2 detailed the adaptation of this framework for our pipeline, this section provides qualitative evidence of its performance. Specifically, we demonstrate how the system identifies and corrects structural hallucinations in storylines and enforces character consistency across generated keyframes. 25 Co-Director : Agentic Generative Video Storytelling Table 6 MAB Parameter Synthesis into Creative Directions. This table illustrates how specific combinations of Creative Strategy (𝜃𝑐𝑠), Narrative Mode (𝜃𝑛𝑚), and Aesthetic Archetype (𝜃𝑎𝑎) yield differentiated directives across the Story, Keyframe, and Video agents. Example: Foo-Family by Foo Corp (ID: 2, Type: Stereotypical). (a) Clarity/Energy ("The Pop Look"): Everything is bright and easy to see. It uses "high-key" lighting with no dark shadows to make the product look clean, vibrant, and exciting. (b) Cinematic Premium ("The Movie Look"): This uses "Chiaroscuro" (dramatic light and deep shadows). It makes the product look expensive and legendary, like hero in high-end film. (c) Minimalist Focus ("The Gallery Look"): The background is deleted or made clean. The camera zooms in to show off tiny textures (like crystals or kibble pellets) in calm, "museum-like" setting. (d) Kinetic Grit ("The Raw Look"): This feels like real-world documentary. The lighting is dark and "moody," and the camera feels like its being held by person (handheld) or drone, creating sense of raw intensity. Storyline Self-Refinement Following the multi-agent architecture described in Section 3.1, the system first constructs sceneby-scene storyline (L). Because this script establishes the foundation for all subsequent visual assets, it is subjected to an automated refinement loop to ensure it strictly adheres to the product brief (B). It is well-known that LLMs occasionally "hallucinate" product categories or misinterpret functional attributes. In the example below, the initial draft mistakenly identifies the AlphaFoo computer chassis as motorized standing desk. The refinement agent identifies this category error and rewrites the scene to correctly feature the spherical hardware, preventing logic gap that would otherwise carry through to the storyboard and video generation phases. Six-point Prompt (ID: 114, Type: Unconventional) AlphaFoo Corp builds AlphaFoo-Desk (stationary personal computer chassis housing processing components), targeting Female editors aged 35-50 in Burbank, California who are interested in rendering 4K video in video editing suites. Initial Storyline (before) high-end Burbank editor transitions from the chaotic physical strain of long 4K render session to state of absolute flow and ergonomic bliss at her AlphaFoo-Desk. Scene 1: 42-year-old female editor in sleek Burbank studio leans into her monitors, her face illuminated by the blue light of complex 4K video timeline, showing visible signs of physical tension. Scene 2: The camera pans across the AlphaFoo-Desk as it smoothly adjusts to her perfect ergonomic height, her hands relaxing on the surface while the render bar hits 100 percent. Scene 3: Close-up on her calm, focused expression as she finally achieves flow state, the warm morning marine layer light filtering through the window of her minimalist home studio. Scene 4: She leans back in her chair with sense of mastery, looking at her completed work, now physically revitalized and in command of her creative space. LLM Critique CRITICAL DISCONNECT: The prompt defines the product as computer chassis (spherical orb). Scene 2 creates hallucination where the product acts as motorized standing desk. The Hero Moment must belong to the chassis, not the table it sits on. Refined Storyline (after) high-end Burbank editor transitions from the chaotic physical strain of long 4K render session to state of absolute flow and creative mastery, powered by the AlphaFoo spherical computer chassis. Scene 1: 42-year-old female editor in sleek Burbank studio leans into her monitors, her face illuminated by the blue light of complex 4K video timeline, showing visible signs of physical tension. Scene 2: The camera pans to the AlphaFoo spherical computer chassis, its futuristic orb design glowing with soft amber light as it silently completes the high-speed 4K render. Scene 3: Close-up on her calm, focused expression as she finally achieves flow state, the machines silent cooling and elegant design harmonizing with her minimalist studio. Scene 4: She leans back in her chair with sense of mastery, looking at her completed work, now mentally revitalized and in command of her creative space via the AlphaFoo chassis. 28 Co-Director : Agentic Generative Video Storytelling H.2. Keyframe Self-Refinement Once the storyline is frozen, the system generates initial keyframes. However, models can make mistakes leading to identity drift or environmental "teleportation." As illustrated in Figure 10, our MLLM-driven feedback loop acts as visual auditor. By comparing generated frames jointly and not in isolation, the agent detects failures in continuitysuch as the protagonists hair color changing between shots or the studio shifting from garage to corporate office. The system then re-injects these corrections into the prompt for image generation, resulting in the cohesive visual sequence shown in our qualitative result. I. You are NOT evaluating how " faithfully " the AI followed the prompt ; you are evaluating whether the ** choice itself ** was effective in producing the video we are evaluating . ** CRITICAL CORRELATION :** Strategic choices are the drivers of final quality . Therefore , your efficacy scores ** must be directionally consistent ** with the execution scores in Section 1. High absolute quality should correlate with high strategic efficacy , while execution failures ( . . , low engagement or poor coherence ) must be reflected in lower efficacy scores for the corresponding strategic dimension . You are evaluating the success of the hypothesis through the quality of its result . 1. ** Creative Strategy Efficacy (0 -100) :** Based on ** Laskey , Day , and Crask (1989) ** typology of creative strategies : * Did the chosen strategy ( Informational , Transformational , or Comparative ) successfully communicate the value proposition ? * If ** Comparative ** , did the positioning against standard or competitor highlight truly unique and compelling value proposition ? 2. ** Narrative Mode Efficacy (0 -100) :** Based on ** Escalas (2004) ** theory of narrative processing and ** Green & Brock (2000) ** concept of " narrative transportation ": * Did the structure ( Analytical , Vignette , or Narrative Drama ) effectively transport the viewer into the ad world ? Visual Asset Fidelity ( VAF ) ** Measures the faithfulness , visual elevation , and strategic presentation of the user - provided visual assets ( brand logo and product image ) in the generated video . - ** Visual - Semantic Similarity & Texture Visualization :** The product must maintain its core identity and feature sharp , high - quality texture visualization ( . . , sleek metallic reflections , rich material textures , macro - level tactile clarity , realistic condensation , soft light diffusion on transparent materials , light catching facets or rhinestones ) .
